title: Kenya Nairobi floods — April 2024
brief_outcome_free: Nairobi, long-rains. Informal settlements along the Nairobi/Mathare river corridors exposed to flash flooding. You are the DOC duty analyst as the forecast window opens.

peak.description: Flood onset / peak — see the Risk Knowledge storyline for the recorded loss & damage.

rounds.title: Early monitoring (2024-04-09)
rounds.engine_state: Antecedent moisture building; ensemble exceedance still modest

rounds.title: Forecast escalation (2024-04-17)
rounds.engine_state: Tail-risk signal rises ahead of onset; DBN carries it forward

evidence_cards.label: Ensemble-mean exceedance prob (ECMWF P_heavy)
evidence_cards.source: ECMWF ensemble P(any member >= 2-yr RP), mean
evidence_cards.teaching_note: The ensemble MEAN can look benign for convective floods. Watch the tail.

evidence_cards.label: Ensemble-max / 2-yr RP (pixel p95)
evidence_cards.teaching_note: Only the tail fires for many flash floods — the signal a mean-threshold system misses.

evidence_cards.label: Yesterday's posterior carried forward (DBN, alpha=0.6)
evidence_cards.teaching_note: Temporal coupling = virtual evidence; keeps the signal elevated near onset.

decision.checkpoint_prompt: The ensemble mean may look benign but the tail signal is rising ahead of onset. What DOC level do you set, and why? A 'Watch + re-check + note the tail uncertainty' is a strong answer.

counterfactual.prompt: What if a precautionary no-regret action (alert river-corridor focal points, pre-position and ready evacuation) were taken at the lead round on the tail signal?
counterfactual.narrative: Acting on a short-lead tail signal is exactly what the flood BN's tail_risk node exists to surface. The counterfactual illustrates loss reduction without claiming certainty.
